#

<!-- 2026.06.22 -->

specific: most useful info at a glance

broad: related items grouped together in a list

# should i put the broader or more specific first in naming

Put the **specific information first** in almost all modern naming systems.

This convention applies to digital files, email subjects, business projects, and URL structures. It ensures that the most unique, actionable detail is visible at a single glance. \[[1](https://theseanamethod.com/2017/01/digital-declutter-day-26-file-naming/)]

### Why Specific First Works Best

- **Improves visibility:** Screen sizes are limited. Putting the specific name first prevents the most important details from getting cut off (truncated) on mobile screens or narrow sidebars.
- **Speeds up scanning:** Human eyes scan the left side of a list. Unique words allow you to find what you need faster than looking at a wall of identical, broad category names.
- **Optimizes search:** Most modern software uses search, not folder browsing. Specific names create highly searchable keywords. \[[1](https://adtribes.io/product-title-optimization-tips/), [2](https://www.butterflygp.com/magazine/best-practices-for-file-naming-every-designer-should-know/), [3](https://blog.shift4shop.com/beginners-guide-to-seo), [4](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/community/microsoft-teams-naming-importance), [5](https://thespotforpardot.com/2017/11/09/things-i-hate-thinking-about-pardot-folder-structure-naming-conventions/)]

### Comparison Examples

| Category              | Broad First (Avoid ❌)              | Specific First (Use )               |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| **File Names**        | 2026\_Marketing\_Report\_Q2\_Draft.pdf  | Q2\_Marketing\_Report\_2026\_Draft.pdf  |
| **Email Subjects**    | HR Department Newsletter June       | June Newsletter - HR Department     |
| **Jira / Tasks**      | Website Redesign - Fix Login Button | Fix Login Button (Website Redesign) |
| **Product Inventory** | Shoes Nike Running Black Size 10    | Nike Black Running Shoes Size 10    |

### The One Exception: Strict Chronological Sorting

The only time you should lead with a broad category is when you are naming files that **must** sort in a perfect timeline. In this case, use the standardized broad-to-specific date format: `YYYY-MM-DD`. \[[1](https://www.mrsharepoint.com/sharepoint-naming-conventions/)]

- _Example:_ `2026-06-10_TaxReturn_Final.pdf`

To help tailor this rule to your needs, tell me:

- **What exactly** are you naming? (e.g., computer files, product SKUs, website pages, or code variables?)

- How will people **mostly find** these items? (e.g., sorting alphabetically, searching via a search bar, or browsing folders?)

## You said: when does broader to more specific fit

Broader-to-more-specific structures fit best when you need to **organize information for systematic sorting, structural navigation, or scientific precision**.

While specific-first is best for quick scanning, broad-first is the standard for building deep organizational frameworks.

### Where Broad-to-Specific Fits Best

- **Folder Trees and Directories:** Your computer's file system relies on this. You open a broad drive, then a category folder, then a specific subfolder.
- **URL Architecture:** Websites rely on hierarchical paths to show search engines how pages relate.
  - _Example:_ `://website.com`
- **Geographic Addresses:** Postal systems require broad-to-specific routing to move mail across the world.
  - _Example:_ Country → State → City → Street → House Number.
- **Scientific Classifications:** Biology uses strict broad-to-specific taxonomies to categorize life.
  - _Example:_ Domain → Kingdom → Phylum → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species. \[[1](https://www.pearson.com/channels/biology/asset/24257276/which-taxonomic-rank-is-the-most-specific), [2](https://www.pearson.com/channels/biology/asset/25527509/which-of-the-following-identifies-the-levels)]
- **Database Relational Tables:** Databases organize data into broad tables before filtering down to specific rows and primary keys.

### Naming Conventions Where Broad-First Rules

You should use broad-first in actual names under three specific conditions:

### 1. Perfect Chronological Sorting

Computer systems sort text alphabetically and numerically from left to right. To keep files in a perfect timeline, you must use the broad-to-specific `YYYY-MM-DD` date format. \[[1](https://prepp.in/question/select-the-correct-option-that-indicates-the-arran-65e06ed3d5a684356e95967e), [2](https://www.torontomu.ca/gcbs/what-we-do/records/records-resources/records-tips-2/)]

- **Use:** `2026-06-10_Invoice_04.pdf`
- **Avoid:** `June-10-2026_Invoice_04.pdf` (This will sort all "June" files together, regardless of the year).

### 2. Grouping Identical Asset Types

If you are managing a massive library of creative or technical assets, leading with the asset type keeps them grouped together when sorted alphabetically.

- **Use:** `Icon_Edit_Blue_16px.png` and `Icon_Save_Blue_16px.png`
- **Result:** All icons stay grouped together, separate from your `Banner_` or `Logo_` files.

### 3. Enterprise Product Inventory (SKUs)

Warehouses and retail inventory systems use broad-to-specific alphanumeric codes to help workers locate items by department first.

- **Use:** `APP-MENS-SHIRT-001` (Apparel → Menswear → Item)

If you are trying to decide which system to build, tell me:

- What is the **total number of items** you expect to manage?

- Will **multiple people** need to add to this naming system, or just you?

## You said: where does more specific to broader fit

More-specific-to-broader structures fit best when **human efficiency, rapid search, and immediate recognition** matter more than rigid system organization.

This layout prioritizes the user's mental focus, giving them the most critical piece of data instantly.

### Where Specific-to-Broad Fits Best

- **Email Subject Lines:** People open emails based on immediate relevance. Putting the core action first prevents it from getting cut off on mobile screens.
  - _Example:_ `Sign Contract by Friday (HR Benefits Update)` \[[1](https://martech.org/a-5-step-blueprint-for-crm-naming-conventions/)]
- **Digital Search Queries:** Search engines and internal database searches are built for specific keywords. Users naturally type the specific item first, then add the category.
  - _Example:_ `iPhone 16 battery replacement tutorial` instead of `Tutorial tech phone apple iPhone 16 battery`.
- **Software Task Management (Jira/Asana/Trello):** On a digital project board, teams need to know exactly what the task is without reading a long prefix.
  - _Example:_ `Fix checkout page crash [E-commerce Redesign]`
- **Customer-Facing Product Names:** E-commerce stores list the brand and defining feature first to capture buyer attention immediately.
  - _Example:_ `Sony WH-1000XM5 Wireless Noise-Canceling Headphones`
- **News and Article Headlines:** Journalism relies on putting the most shocking or important detail in the first three words to grab attention.
  - _Example:_ `Gold Medal Won by Underdog Team at Finals`

### Why This Format Dominates Digital Spaces

### 1. It Outsmarts "Truncation"

Digital interfaces have limited horizontal space. When text is too long, computers cut it off with an ellipsis (...). By putting the specific details first, you ensure the user sees the unique identifier even if the rest of the name is hidden.

### 2. It Prevents Visual Fatigue

If a user is looking at a list of 50 files that all start with the broad category `Marketing_2026_Project_...`, their eyes have to jump to the middle of every single line to find the right file. Starting with the specific name allows them to scan straight down the left margin.

To help you choose the right direction, tell me:

- Are you designing this system for **internal machine automation** or for **human eyes to read daily**?

- What is the **exact name of one item** you are struggling to structure right now?

## You said: on window title

For window titles, always use **specific-to-broad** naming.

Put the immediate, active piece of information first (like the document name or specific task), and place the broad application or company name last. \[[1](https://www.snow.dog/blog/how-to-use-accessible-names-properly-to-easily-improve-accessibility-of-your-website-aria-label-aria-labeledby-and-more)]

### The Standard Formula

> **\[Specific Page/Document] — \[Broad App/Website Name]**

- **Good :** `Q2 Budget Draft — Microsoft Excel`
- **Bad ❌:** `Microsoft Excel — Q2 Budget Draft`
- **Good :** `Inbox (3) — john.doe@email.com`
- **Bad ❌:** `Email Client — john.doe@email.com — Inbox (3)`

### Why Specific-First is Crucial for Window Titles

- **Taskbar Scanning:** Operating system taskbars (like Windows or macOS) have very narrow tabs. If you open five spreadsheets and the window titles start with "Microsoft Excel...", every single tab will look identical. You won't know which is which without clicking.
- **Browser Tab Readability:** Web browsers aggressively truncate (cut off) text. A user with 15 tabs open might only see the first 10 characters. Leading with the specific page title ensures they can still navigate their tabs. \[[1](https://www.presslabs.com/how-to/optimize-page-titles/), [2](https://www.getstark.co/wcag-explained/operable/navigable/page-titled/)]
- **Accessibility (Screen Readers):** Visually impaired users relying on screen readers hear text from left to right. Announcing the specific page name first allows them to immediately identify the window without listening to the app name every single time they switch tasks. \[[1](https://milivcounty.gov/communications/creating-ada-compliant-documents/)]

To help refine your window title design, let me know:

- Is this for a **web application**, a **desktop software**, or a **public website**?

- What is an **example of a page or document** a user would open in it?
